topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I stared at the printed email, the words blurring into an infuriating mess. Sarah. My supposed confidante, the one who always shared every detail of her life, had been telling me for months she was a senior architect at a prestigious firm. Today, I found the official confirmation email, a stark, undeniable document that detailed a completely different role—a junior administrative assistant at a struggling local bookstore. My jaw tightened until a dull ache bloomed behind my molars. I crumpled the paper, the sound sharp in the quiet apartment. When she finally called, her voice was annoyingly bright, full of rehearsed apologies. I just stood up, pacing the length of the living room, my hands clenched so tightly my knuckles were white. "I just... I didn't want to bother you," I managed, my voice dangerously level. "It’s just... everything you told me is a fabrication."

[story 2]

I watched her across the crowded cafe table, the easy confidence radiating off her like heat. She was describing her 'exciting new project,' her voice pitched high with manufactured enthusiasm. Then, a detail slipped out—a specific client name, a project timeline—that I had seen documented online, something I knew she couldn't possibly know unless she was lying about her actual position. A cold, hard knot formed in my stomach, quickly escalating into a tight, hot pressure behind my eyes. I stopped stirring my tea, the spoon hovering mid-air. I leaned forward, lowering my voice so only she could hear, and the words came out clipped and sharp. "Stop," I said. It wasn't a request; it was an absolute command. Her smile faltered, a flicker of panic crossing her features before she plastered the usual charm back on. The air between us felt thick, charged with unspoken accusation. I didn't wait for a response; I simply pushed my chair back, the scrape of the wood against the tile echoing the sudden, violent shift in my internal state.

[story 3]

The evidence was undeniable, sitting on my desk like a misplaced accusation. I reread the correspondence, each line a fresh stab of resentment. It wasn't just a small omission; it was a complete restructuring of the narrative she had built around herself. A slow, building tremor ran through my hands as I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over her contact information. Every muscle in my body seemed coiled, ready to snap. When she finally admitted it during our late-night call, her explanation was flimsy, riddled with excuses that sounded hollow even to my own ears. I listened, my breathing shallow and rapid. When she finished, I didn't speak immediately. Instead, I walked into the kitchen, grabbed a glass of water, and drank it down in one gulp, the cold liquid doing little to quell the internal combustion raging within me. I set the glass down with deliberate force, the clink sounding deafening in the silence that followed. "You have no right to treat me like an idiot," I stated, my tone flat, devoid of any warmth.
